mainText:
Good morning. Here's your Breeze communications briefing for Sunday, April 5th.

There is no new Breeze coverage in the last 24 hours. Breeze remains clear of the news cycle over the Easter holiday weekend — no crisis flags, no negative coverage — as the spring West Coast expansion continues operating and the upcoming Tampa international service to Costa Rica and the Dominican Republic stays as a positive backdrop story in local markets.

Now for industry and competitor news.

Easter Saturday brought one of the worst aviation disruption days of 2026, with the F.A.A. tracking nearly three hundred forty cancellations and more than thirty-five hundred delays cascading through major hub airports — Chicago O'Hare recorded more than three hundred disruptions, Miami International nearly two hundred, Dallas-Fort Worth over one hundred twenty, and Newark Liberty approximately one hundred thirty — as a severe Midwest thunderstorm system compounded by T.S.A. staffing gaps from the government-shutdown-driven wave of officer resignations created a rolling operational cascade that stranded tens of thousands of Easter travelers across the country. Breeze's point-to-point model routing passengers through secondary markets without connecting hub exposure kept our operation largely clear of the meltdown, and communications should consider a timely social media post or earned-media outreach this weekend making the case that Breeze travelers avoided exactly this kind of Easter chaos.

Aviation A2Z reported yesterday that the structural underpinnings of Delta's two-decade run as America's most admired and operationally reliable airline are showing meaningful stress, with industry analysts pointing to a widening product quality gap, leadership questions, and United Airlines' calculated investment in transatlantic routes and premium cabin upgrades as evidence that the competitive distance between the two carriers is closing faster than the industry expected. Network planning should establish a regular tracking cadence for Delta's upcoming capacity announcements — any Delta pullback from leisure or mid-size markets to shore up its premium transatlantic positioning would be an opportunity signal for Breeze to accelerate marketing in those cities.

Aviation A2Z also reported yesterday that United Airlines' flight attendant union leadership unanimously voted to advance a new labor contract to the full membership for ratification, with voting scheduled from April twenty-third through mid-May; under the proposed deal, first-year United flight attendants would start at nearly thirty-seven dollars per hour on the date of signing and rise to over forty-two dollars within four years, while senior flight attendants with thirteen or more years of service would approach ninety-seven dollars per hour. As United's labor costs accelerate alongside the bag fee hikes and fuel surcharges the carrier already pushed through earlier this month, Breeze's non-union operating structure and lean cost model remain a meaningful structural cost advantage worth actively highlighting in upcoming investor presentations and analyst conversations.

That's your briefing. Roger is standing by when you're ready to dive deeper or take action on any of these stories.
